stem: What is the default maximum speed for a car inside a built-up area where no signs say otherwise?

explanation.short: Inside a built-up area, the default maximum for cars is 50 km/h.
explanation.examTip: Many residential and shopping streets are signed at 30 km/h, which overrides the default.

stem: Outside a built-up area on a normal road, what is the default maximum speed for a car?

explanation.short: On normal roads outside a built-up area, the default for cars is 80 km/h.
explanation.examTip: Expressways (autoweg) raise this to 100 km/h; motorways higher still where signed.

stem: On a Dutch motorway with no signed exception, what is the maximum speed for a car between 06:00 and 19:00?

explanation.short: Between 06:00 and 19:00, cars are capped at 100 km/h on motorways unless a sign says otherwise.
explanation.examTip: Outside daytime hours, the limit follows the signed value, which is often 120 or 130 km/h.

stem: In a woonerf (residential yard zone), what speed must you drive at?

explanation.short: In a woonerf you must drive at walking pace, roughly 15 km/h, and yield to pedestrians using the whole street.
explanation.examTip: Pedestrians, and especially playing children, have priority over vehicles in a woonerf.

stem: If you double your speed, what happens to the reaction distance (assuming the same reaction time)?

explanation.short: Reaction distance grows in proportion to speed, so doubling speed roughly doubles it.
explanation.examTip: Reaction distance is fixed by your reaction time; only braking distance grows with the square of speed.

stem: If you double your speed, what happens to the braking distance on the same road and tyres?

explanation.short: Braking distance grows with the square of speed, so doubling speed roughly quadruples it.
explanation.examTip: This is why a small increase from 50 to 60 km/h adds far more stopping distance than it looks.

stem: Total stopping distance is best described as:

explanation.short: Total stopping distance is the sum of how far you travel while reacting and how far you travel while braking.
explanation.examTip: Both components grow with speed, but braking grows much faster — so high speeds dominate the total.

stem: Compared to a dry road, what is the most realistic effect of a wet road on braking distance?

explanation.short: A wet road can roughly double braking distance compared to dry tarmac.
explanation.examTip: Worn tyres make the effect worse — reduce speed in advance rather than relying on hard braking.

stem: In dense fog, how should you choose your speed?

choices.text: Drive at the signed limit because it is the legal speed
Drive slowly enough to stop within the distance you can see to be clear
Drive faster to leave the fog quickly
Drive at half the signed limit regardless

explanation.short: Your speed must always let you stop within the distance you can see to be clear, even well below the signed limit.
explanation.examTip: Speed limits are upper bounds — conditions can require a much lower speed.

stem: In dry conditions, what is a common rule of thumb for safe following distance behind the car in front?

choices.text: Close enough to read the number plate

explanation.short: Roughly a two-second gap is the standard guideline in dry conditions.
explanation.examTip: Double the gap on wet roads, and extend it further in poor visibility or behind heavy vehicles.

stem: What is the standard daytime (06:00–19:00) speed limit on Dutch motorways for cars?

explanation.short: Since 2020 the default daytime motorway limit is 100 km/h; higher limits apply only where signed and only at night.
explanation.examTip: Always obey the signed limit — it overrides the default in either direction.

stem: You see the rectangular blue sign that marks a built-up area. From that point the default speed limit is:

explanation.short: Inside a built-up area the default limit is 50 km/h unless lower limits (30 km/h zones) are signed.
explanation.examTip: Leaving the area is shown by the same sign with a red diagonal bar — default rises to 80 km/h.

stem: Stopping distance is made up of which two parts?

explanation.short: Total stopping distance = reaction distance (before the brakes act) + braking distance (while the brakes act).
explanation.examTip: Fatigue, alcohol and phone use mainly stretch the reaction part, not the brake part.

stem: If you double your speed, the braking distance on dry tarmac becomes roughly:

explanation.short: Braking distance grows with the square of speed, so doubling speed roughly quadruples it.
explanation.examTip: Reaction distance only doubles — but the squared effect on braking is what makes high-speed crashes severe.

stem: It is raining and the road surface is wet. How should you adjust your following distance compared to dry conditions?

choices.text: Reduce it slightly to keep up

explanation.short: On a wet road grip drops, so roughly double the two-second guideline to about four seconds.
explanation.examTip: On ice or hard-packed snow, an even larger gap (eight to ten seconds) is reasonable.

stem: Another driver is tailgating you on a single-lane road. What is the safest response?

choices.text: Brake hard to make them back off
Keep your speed and increase the gap to the car in front
Speed up well above the limit
Wave them past while turning around

explanation.short: Hold your line, leave more space ahead, and let them overtake when it is safe.
explanation.examTip: Brake-checking a tailgater is dangerous and may be treated as reckless driving.

stem: At 14:00 on a clear, dry day you drive on a Dutch motorway where the signed maximum is 130 km/h. What is your legal maximum speed?

choices.text: 130 km/h, because that is the signed maximum
100 km/h, because of the daytime cap between 06:00 and 19:00

explanation.short: Between 06:00 and 19:00 cars are capped at 100 km/h regardless of higher signed limits.
explanation.examTip: The 130 km/h sign only applies at night — 100 km/h is the legal max during the day.

stem: On a dry road at 50 km/h your braking distance is about 12 m. You drive the same route at the same speed in heavy rain. Roughly what is the braking distance now?

choices.text: About 12 m — speed has not changed
About 24 m — wet roads roughly double braking distance
About 6 m — water lubricates the brakes
About 50 m — wet roads quadruple braking distance

explanation.short: Wet roads roughly double the braking distance; reaction distance is unchanged.
explanation.examTip: Doubling speed quadruples braking; wet roads double the braking part again.

stem: It has just started raining after a dry week. You are following a lorry at 80 km/h with a 2-second gap. What should you do?

choices.text: Keep the 2-second gap — it is correct for any speed
Drop back to at least 4 seconds because of wet road and lorry's blocked view
Move closer to use the lorry as a windbreak
Overtake immediately — the first rain is always brief

explanation.short: First-rain conditions roughly double braking distance; behind a heavy vehicle, leave even more gap.
explanation.examTip: Wet: double the gap to 4 seconds; behind a lorry, add more because their bulk blocks your view ahead.

stem: You are driving in fog. Visibility drops to about 40 m. Which lights may you legally use?

choices.text: Main beam only — to cut through the fog
Dipped headlights and front fog lights only
Dipped headlights, front fog lights, and rear fog lights

explanation.short: Front fog lights below 200 m visibility; rear fog lights only below 50 m. At 40 m visibility both are allowed.
explanation.examTip: Switch rear fog lights off as soon as visibility improves — they dazzle the driver behind you.
